text:
The sea.

There is something in mountains that flatters human vanity. They make us small, yes, but they also make us climbers, conquerors, people with goals and summits. We photograph ourselves at the top. Mountains have edges, peaks, a grammar we can read and follow.

The sea refuses all of that. It has no top. It has no path. It simply continues, in every direction, at every depth, without caring in the slightest whether you are there. Its beauty is not the beauty of a thing that can be admired from a distance but of something you must enter, and once you enter it, it enters you back, cold and salt and utterly indifferent. There is a particular freedom in being somewhere that does not acknowledge your presence.

And then there is the sound. Mountains are largely silent, which is its own gift. But the sea speaks constantly, in a language that is almost language, that keeps almost arriving at meaning and then retreating again. People have been falling asleep to that sound since before they were people. It reaches something older than thought.

The sea also contains multitudes in a way mountains cannot match. Mountains are what they appear to be. The sea is surface and then fathoms, coral and trench and darkness and creatures that make their own light in places where no light has ever fallen from the sky. Most of the living world, by volume, is in that water. Mountains are stone. The sea is alive all the way down.

And at dawn, or at dusk, when the light moves across open water, there is nothing else quite like it on earth. The whole sky doubles. The world becomes pure color and motion. A mountain at sunset is beautiful. The sea at sunset makes you forget, briefly, who you are.

That forgetting is the loveliest thing of all.
